source_article: A company spokesperson claims, 'Our product is safe, as shown by independent studies.' They only cite the three studies that found no harm, while ignoring the dozen that found serious side effects.
Person A: 'No politician ever keeps their promises.' Person B: 'Mayor Johnson lowered taxes like he promised.' Person A: 'Well, he's not a true politician then.'

Person A: 'If you can show me one peer-reviewed study proving the drug works, I'll believe you.' After being shown the study, Person A says, 'That study was funded by the pharma company, so it's biased.'
A journalist asks, 'Why do you continue to support a corrupt regime?'
An employee says, 'I know stealing from the office is wrong, but I had a family emergency and no other choice, so it's okay this time.'
A believer states, 'There's no evidence that ghosts don't exist, therefore they do.'
A movie critic wrote, 'This film is a masterpiece of disappointment.' The advertisement quotes only 'a masterpiece'.
The car salesman says, 'This SUV gets 50 miles per gallon,' omitting that it's only highway mileage; city is 25.
A televangelist points to a cluster of reported healings in one city as proof of divine intervention, ignoring all the cities where no healings occurred.
A creationist argues, 'I just can't believe that random mutations could produce something as complex as the bacterial flagellum, so it must have been designed.'
Someone says, 'The cancer suddenly disappeared and doctors can't explain it, therefore it's a miracle from God.'
A politician claims, 'Under my leadership, employment is up!', citing only the months where employment rose, while ignoring months where it fell.
A parent says, 'No true teenager would ever enjoy classical music.' When told about a teen who loves Mozart, the parent replies, 'Then he's not a true teenager.'

A skeptic says, 'I'll accept that climate change is real if you can show me five winters colder than the average.' After five cold winters are shown, they say, 'That's just weather, I need a global average over a century.'
At a press conference, a reporter asks, 'When did you stop accepting bribes?'
A student argues, 'I deserve a higher grade because I worked harder than anyone else, even if my answers were wrong.'
A conspiracy theorist claims, 'No one has disproven that the moon landing was faked, so it probably was.'
A politician's speech said, 'We must not, under any circumstances, abandon our allies.' The opponent's ad quotes only 'abandon our allies', removing the 'must not'.
The nutritional label highlights '0g trans fat per serving', but the serving size is unrealistically small and the product contains partially hydrogenated oil.
A gambler notices that the roulette ball landed on black five times in a row and declares, 'The wheel must be rigged!', ignoring that such streaks are statistically normal.
A parent tells a child, 'I can't understand how the tides work; they must be caused by a giant sea creature.'
A politician states, 'The facts show that our policies are working. GDP grew 4% last year.' He doesn't mention that inflation also hit a 20-year high.
A person says, 'A real vegan would never wear leather.' When told about a vegan who owns a vintage leather jacket, they respond, 'Then they're not a real vegan.'
An anti-vaxxer says, 'If vaccines are so safe, show me a study that proves they cause no harm for 50 years.' After a 30-year study is presented, they say, 'That's not 50 years; you need a full 50.'
A talk-show host asks a guest, 'Why do you hate freedom?'
A driver who was caught speeding tells the officer, 'You should let me off because my wife is in labor, even though I was speeding before I knew she was in labor.'
A believer in psychics says, 'You can't prove that they can't read minds, so they might be able to.'
A CEO's memo read, 'We are not currently considering layoffs, but we cannot rule out the possibility if the market worsens.' The union newsletter quoted: 'We cannot rule out layoffs.'
The travel brochure boasts 'steps from the beach', but it's a 20-minute walk and you need to cross a highway.
A pseudo-historian points to three ancient ruins that form a straight line on a map and claims this proves an advanced lost civilization, ignoring the millions of ruins that don't align.
A person argues, 'I can't imagine how the human eye could have evolved step by step; therefore it must have been specially created.'
After surviving a plane crash, a passenger says, 'It's a divine miracle that I'm alive,' ignoring the skill of the pilots and modern engineering.
A supplement company claims, 'Our product improves memory,' and cites only a single, small, unblinded study, ignoring several larger studies that found no effect.
A nationalist says, 'No true patriot would question our country's foreign policy.' When someone points to a decorated veteran who is a vocal critic, they say, 'He's no true patriot.'

An employer says, 'I'll promote you if you increase sales by 20% this quarter.' When the employee increases sales by 25%, the employer says, 'Well, the market was up this quarter, so it wasn't really your effort.'
A pundit asks, 'How can you defend a system that oppresses minorities?' (when the system's oppression has not been established)
A homeowner says, 'The no-parking ordinance shouldn't apply to me because my driveway is being repaved.'
A flat-earther asserts, 'No one has ever shown me the curve, therefore the Earth is flat.'
A book was reviewed as 'an impressive display of the author's ignorance.' The publisher's website features the blurb 'an impressive display.'
An energy drink ad says, 'Contains electrolytes!', but doesn't mention that a typical banana has more, and the drink is loaded with sugar.
A stock analyst picks a portfolio of 100 stocks and then, after a year, highlights the five that performed exceptionally well, claiming his 'method' is successful.
A person says, 'I can't conceive of a reality where there's no afterlife; therefore, an afterlife must exist.'
A patient claims, 'The prayer circle cured my infection,' despite taking antibiotics at the same time.
A candidate says, 'I have a plan to reduce crime,' and provides statistics for one city where crime dropped, omitting that crime rose in three other cities that implemented the same plan.
Person A: 'No professional chef would use a microwave.' Person B: 'But Gordon Ramsay uses one occasionally.' Person A: 'Then he's not a true professional chef.'
In a debate, one side says, 'If you're so sure, name just one reliable source.' After a source is named, they reply, 'That source is obviously part of the conspiracy; I need an independent one.'

A believer in astrology says, 'Until science can explain why the positions of planets affect personality, I say it's true.'

A technophobe says, 'I simply cannot believe that a computer can think; therefore, artificial intelligence is impossible.'
A person sees a face-like pattern on a water stain and concludes it must be a supernatural sign, ignoring the tendency of humans to see faces.
A dieter claims, 'The keto diet is the only healthy way,' and only shares testimonials from people who lost weight, never mentioning those who developed health issues.
A group member says, 'A real feminist supports all women.' When another feminist criticizes a female politician, the first says, 'Well, she must not be a real feminist.'
A film critic says, 'I'll concede the movie is good if it wins an Oscar.' When it wins an Oscar, they say, 'The Oscars are political; it doesn't mean anything.'
A reporter asks a politician, 'What do you say to those who claim you laundered money?' (without evidence that such claims are credible)
A corporation argues that environmental regulations should not apply to them because they are the sole employer in a poor region.
A ghost hunter says, 'We can't explain these cold spots, hence they are spirits.'
The scientist wrote, 'Results were not statistically significant, but suggested a possible trend.' The press release stated, 'Study suggests a strong trend.'
A product claims to be 'made with real fruit', but the ingredient list shows it contains only fruit juice concentrate as the fourth ingredient, after sugar, flour, and oil.
A fan of a sports team says, 'We lost because the referee was biased,' ignoring that their team committed more fouls and missed key shots.
A conspiracy theorist says, 'I don't believe the official story because I can't imagine how planes could bring down those towers.'
A person who recovered from an illness after visiting a shrine attributes it to a miracle, not the treatment they received at the clinic nearby.
A study is cited as proving that homework improves grades, but the quoted researchers only studied high-SES students and acknowledged the limitation; the limitation is omitted.
Person A: 'No genuine gamer plays on mobile.' Person B: 'I know a professional esports player who competes on mobile.' Person A: 'That's not real gaming.'

A panelist asks, 'Why is your party always obstructing progress?' (presupposing that any opposition is obstruction, not legitimate disagreement)
A student plagiarized but says, 'I shouldn't be expelled because I'm a scholarship athlete and bring prestige to the school.'
A trade union leader says, 'Management hasn't provided a single document disproving our wage theft claim, so they must be guilty.'

A politician's speech originally said, 'My opponent has some good ideas, but overall his plan is flawed.' The campaign ad quotes only 'My opponent has some good ideas' to make it seem like an endorsement.
The label says 'Now with 50% less fat!' but the original product was very high in fat, so the new version is still high, and it's higher in sugar.
A marketing campaign for a lottery says, 'Someone in this town won big!' not revealing that thousands of others in the same town lost money.
A prescientific text claims that lightning is a sign of an angry deity. The author says, 'I cannot comprehend how a natural force could be so powerful; it must be divine wrath.'
After a natural disaster, a televangelist claims it was God's punishment, citing a passage from scripture that he interprets to fit the event, ignoring any contradictory interpretations.
An advertiser says, '9 out of 10 dentists recommend our toothpaste,' but the survey allowed dentists to choose multiple brands and only those who already recommended it were counted.
'No true musician would use Auto-Tune.' 'But many Grammy winners use it.' 'They're not true musicians then.'

A skeptic says, 'I'll believe in psychic powers if a psychic can tell me something only I know.' After the psychic succeeds, the skeptic says, 'They must have researched me; use a completely anonymous test.'
A lawyer asks a witness, 'When did you stop embezzling funds from the company?'
A wealthy individual says, 'I shouldn't have to pay taxes because I already create jobs,' though their company has been laying off workers.
A creationist argues, 'Since we have no uncontested transitional fossil between fish and amphibian, evolution is in doubt.'
A memoir reads, 'It was, as the saying goes, the worst of times.' A critic quotes just 'the worst of times' to claim the author is purely negative.
The 'unlimited data' plan is advertised, but the fine print says speeds are throttled after 2GB to unusable levels.
A politican cites the drop in unemployment from 8% to 6% as proof the economy is booming, but ignores that the drop was primarily due to people leaving the workforce.
A person says, 'I just find it impossible that the Earth is billions of years old, so it must be only a few thousand.'
A fisherman says, 'I caught a huge fish after praying to Saint Peter; it was a miracle,' forgetting all the times he prayed and didn't catch anything.
The school board claims 'test scores have risen dramatically' by comparing only the top-performing class from this year to the lowest from five years ago.
'A true patriot never questions the military.' 'My brother, who served three tours, is critical of some missions.' 'He's been brainwashed; a true patriot wouldn't say that.'
A manager says, 'If you can bring in 5 new clients, I'll make you partner.' After the employee brings 5, the manager says, 'We need clients that spend more; quality over quantity.'
A journalist asks a candidate, 'How do you respond to allegations of corruption?' when no formal allegations have been made.
A defendant argues, 'I shouldn't be held to the same self-defense standard because I was in fear for my life, even though I started the fight.'
A U.F.O. enthusiast says, 'The government hasn't released everything, so they must be hiding proof of aliens.'
The company memo said, 'While there are some concerns, overall the project is a success.' The whistleblower's blog quotes only 'there are some concerns' as if the project is failing.
A cereal box says 'whole grain' in large letters, but the first ingredient is sugar and the whole grain is just a small amount of oat flour.
A sports analyst picks 50 different teams to win the championship and then, when one does, claims his analysis was on point, ignoring the 49 wrong predictions.
A person says, 'The idea that we are just chemical machines is so unromantic and unbelievable, therefore we have a soul.'
A statue of a deity is said to weep oil. The faithful claim it's a sign, ignoring the investigation that found a hidden pipe.
A researcher claims a new therapy is effective because 70% of participants improved, but conveniently omits that the control group showed 65% improvement.
'No real democrat would ever vote against a social program.' 'But Senator X, a lifelong democrat, voted against one.' 'Then he's not a real democrat.'

An ad says 'Up to 100% fiber!' but the average product has only 10%, and only one variant, which is impossible to find, reaches 100%.
A self-help guru says, 'The secret law of attraction worked for me,' listing only the times when positive thinking coincided with success, never the failures.
A person argues, 'I can't see how the Grand Canyon could be carved by a little river over millions of years, so a great flood must have done it quickly.'
